executiveSummary:
**Executive Summary: Medicaid Program Performance Analysis**

National Medicaid enrollment as of January 2026 totals 75.3 million beneficiaries, with most major states experiencing enrollment declines following the end of continuous coverage protections. California leads with 12.6 million enrollees despite a significant 6.2% year-over-year decrease, while Michigan shows the steepest decline at 7.6%. Texas maintains the third-largest program with 4.0 million enrollees and a moderate 3.1% decline, closely mirroring Pennsylvania's reduction rate. Notably, North Carolina stands as the only major state showing growth at 0.5%, suggesting effective retention strategies worth examining. National spending reached $215.9 billion in fiscal year 2024, with a per-enrollee average of $2,869, though significant variation exists across states—from Florida's efficient $2,031 per enrollee to Pennsylvania's high-cost $4,377 per enrollee.

Texas demonstrates mixed performance relative to peer states, spending $2,988 per enrollee—slightly above the national average but remaining cost-competitive among large states. However, quality outcomes present a concerning picture, with Texas scoring 62 out of 100 on composite quality measures, ranking 30th nationally and falling into the "moderate" performance tier. This places Texas below peer states like California and New York, while states such as Massachusetts (81) and Minnesota (80) demonstrate significantly superior quality performance. Texas does excel in managed care implementation with 93.3% penetration, well above the 83.2% national average, suggesting strong infrastructure for coordinated care delivery. Leadership should prioritize quality improvement initiatives while leveraging the state's managed care capabilities, particularly as federal oversight activities remain active and new HCBS quality measures are being implemented for 2028.
texasSpotlight:
## Texas Medicaid Program Performance Analysis

Texas operates the nation's third-largest Medicaid program with 4.03 million enrollees, representing a substantial 5.4% of national enrollment. The state's per-enrollee spending of $2,988 falls above the national average of $2,869 but remains competitive among large states—notably outperforming California ($2,280) and Florida ($2,031) while spending significantly less than expansion states like New York ($3,431) and Pennsylvania ($4,377). Texas demonstrates strong operational efficiency through its managed care model, with 93.3% penetration exceeding the national average of 83.2%, suggesting effective cost containment and care coordination. The state's enrollment declined 3.1% year-over-year, closely tracking the national trend as states unwind pandemic-era continuous coverage protections, indicating stable program management during this transition period.

However, Texas faces notable quality challenges that warrant close monitoring. With a quality score of 62/100, the state ranks 30th nationally and falls into the "moderate" performance tier—below the national average of 65.6 but avoiding the bottom quintile occupied by neighboring southern states like Arkansas (54) and Oklahoma (51). This positioning among non-expansion southern peers suggests systemic regional challenges in care delivery and outcomes. The state's substantial total expenditure of $12.05 billion, ranking fourth nationally, underscores the program's fiscal significance and the imperative for quality improvements. Key areas to watch include whether Texas can leverage its strong managed care infrastructure to drive quality gains while maintaining cost discipline, and how effectively the state navigates ongoing federal oversight activities as indicated by active HHS OIG monitoring and evolving CMS regulatory requirements around HCBS quality measures.

alerts.title: Pennsylvania Per-Enrollee Spending 53% Above National Average
alerts.description: Pennsylvania's per-enrollee spending of $4,377 is significantly higher than the national average of $2,869, indicating potential cost management issues.

alerts.title: Florida Exceptionally Low Per-Enrollee Spending Raises Concerns
alerts.description: Florida's per-enrollee spending of $2,031 is 29% below the national average, potentially indicating inadequate care provision or access issues.

alerts.title: Five States Show Critically Low Quality Performance
alerts.description: Arkansas, South Carolina, Alabama, Oklahoma, and Mississippi all scored below 55 on quality measures, well under the national average of 65.6.

alerts.title: Massachusetts Achieves Exceptional Quality-Spending Balance
alerts.description: Massachusetts combines the highest quality score (81) with reasonable per-enrollee spending of $4,201, demonstrating effective program management.

executiveInsights.title: Texas quality score at risk: 62/100 places state in bottom 40%
executiveInsights.summary: Texas ranks #30 of 50 states with a quality score of 62/100, falling into the 'moderate' tier but dangerously close to the low performer threshold of <60. Only 15 states currently score below 60, putting Texas at risk of sliding into poor performance.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Quality scores directly impact federal funding, CMS oversight, and contract renewals. Being near the low performer threshold creates regulatory risk and potential intervention.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Flag quality improvement as urgent priority to HHSC leadership

executiveInsights.title: Texas per-enrollee costs 4% above national average despite efficiency focus
executiveInsights.summary: Texas spends $2,988 per enrollee compared to the national average of $2,869, representing a $119 premium per member. With 4M+ enrollees, this translates to nearly $480M in above-average spending annually.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Cost efficiency is a key contract performance metric and budget pressure point for HHSC. The variance suggests potential optimization opportunities or service delivery inefficiencies.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Conduct cost driver analysis comparing Texas to efficient states like FL ($2,031) and NC ($2,616)

executiveInsights.title: Texas managed care penetration leads nation at 93.3% vs 83.2% average
executiveInsights.summary: Texas achieves 93.3% managed care penetration with 3.86M MC enrollees, significantly outperforming the 83.2% national average. This represents strong program maturity and potential cost management capability.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: High MC penetration demonstrates operational excellence and creates opportunities to showcase Texas as a best practice model for other state contracts and federal stakeholders.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Position Texas MC success as proof point in upcoming procurement opportunities

executiveInsights.title: CMS introducing new HCBS quality measures for 2028 implementation
executiveInsights.summary: Federal Register shows CMS finalizing 2028 Medicaid Home and Community-Based Services Quality Measure Set. This represents new compliance requirements and measurement standards coming online.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: New quality measures will impact Texas's already concerning quality score positioning and require system updates, data collection changes, and potentially new reporting capabilities.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review HCBS quality measure requirements for Texas compliance gaps

executiveInsights.title: Texas enrollment decline accelerating: -3.1% YoY impacts revenue base
executiveInsights.summary: Texas lost 3.1% of Medicaid enrollees year-over-year, representing approximately 128K fewer members. This decline aligns with national unwinding trends but impacts total program revenue and economies of scale.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Enrollment declines affect contract economics and may require operational rightsizing or service delivery model adjustments to maintain profitability and performance standards.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Model enrollment trend impact on contract financials and staffing requirements

riskOpportunity.label: Enrollment decline acceleration
riskOpportunity.description: Texas Medicaid enrollment declining 3.1% YoY amid post-PHE unwinding, creating operational complexity and revenue pressure.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Develop enrollment stabilization and retention analytics capabilities to support HHSC redetermination processes.

riskOpportunity.label: Quality measure gap
riskOpportunity.description: Texas ranks #30 nationally with 62/100 quality score, creating vulnerability in federal oversight and value-based contracts.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Position quality improvement consulting services targeting Texas's gap to national average performance.

riskOpportunity.label: HHSC procurement opportunities
riskOpportunity.description: Active Texas HHSC procurement pipeline presents contract expansion and new service delivery opportunities.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Systematically monitor HHSC procurement portal and position for high-value technology modernization RFPs.

riskOpportunity.label: HHS OIG oversight intensification
riskOpportunity.description: Active OIG Medicaid portfolio oversight creates compliance risk but positions Accenture's audit readiness capabilities.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Strengthen compliance monitoring and audit preparation services to preempt OIG findings.

riskOpportunity.label: HCBS quality measures expansion
riskOpportunity.description: New 2028 HCBS quality measure requirements create implementation challenges and consulting revenue opportunities.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Develop HCBS quality measurement and reporting solutions for Texas managed care organizations.

riskOpportunity.label: Managed care recompete cycle
riskOpportunity.description: Texas's 93.3% managed care penetration suggests upcoming MCO contract renewals requiring systems integration support.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Engage early with Texas MCOs and HHSC on next-generation managed care procurement strategy.

riskOpportunity.label: Per-enrollee cost pressure
riskOpportunity.description: Texas $2,988 per-enrollee spend above national average creates budget pressure and efficiency improvement needs.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Propose cost containment analytics and program integrity solutions to reduce per-member costs.

riskOpportunity.label: Competitive positioning erosion
riskOpportunity.description: Multi-state Medicaid competitors expanding footprint while Texas market faces regulatory and operational headwinds.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Accelerate Texas-specific solution development and strengthen HHSC relationship management.

pulseMetrics.label: Total Enrollment
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas Medicaid serves 4,032,864 enrollees as of January 2026.

pulseMetrics.label: 12-Mo Enrollment Delta
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas enrollment declined 3.1% year-over-year, slightly better than national average decline.

pulseMetrics.label: Per Enrollee Spend
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas spends $2,988 per enrollee annually, below the national average of $2,869.

pulseMetrics.label: Managed Care Rate
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas has 93.3% managed care penetration, significantly above the national average of 83.2%.

pulseMetrics.label: Quality Score
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas scored 62 out of 100 on quality measures, ranking 30th nationally in moderate tier.

pulseMetrics.label: Active Procurement Items
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Two active procurement portals identified: Texas HHSC opportunities and Electronic State Business Daily.

pulseMetrics.label: OIG/Regulatory Signals
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Three OIG and regulatory items identified including active oversight docket and work plan items.

pulseMetrics.label: Next Milestone
pulseMetrics.tooltip: No specific milestone data available in current dataset.
